records.ety: [F. abuser; L. abusus, p. p. of abuti to abuse, misuse; ab + uti to use. See Use.]
records.morph: [imp. & p. p. Abused (<?/); p. pr. & vb. n. Abusing.]

defs: To put to a wrong use; to misapply; to misuse; to put to a bad use; to use for a wrong purpose or end; to pervert; as, to abuse inherited gold; to make an excessive use of; as, to abuse one's authority.
quotes: This principle (if one may so abuse the word) shoots rapidly into popularity.

defs: To use ill; to maltreat; to act injuriously to; to punish or to tax excessively; to hurt; as, to abuse prisoners, to abuse one's powers, one's patience.
To revile; to reproach coarsely; to disparage.
quotes: The . . . tellers of news abused the general.
Their eyes red and staring, cozened with a moist cloud, and abused by a double object.

records.ety: [F. abus, L. abusus, fr. abuti. See Abuse, v. t.]
usage: -- Abuse, Invective. Abuse is generally prompted by anger, and vented in harsh and unseemly words. It is more personal and coarse than invective. Abuse generally takes place in private quarrels; invective in writing or public discussions. Invective may be conveyed in refined language and dictated by indignation against what is blameworthy. C. J. Smith.

defs: Improper treatment or use; application to a wrong or bad purpose; misuse; as, an abuse of our natural powers; an abuse of civil rights, or of privileges or advantages; an abuse of language.
quotes: Liberty may be endangered by the abuses of liberty, as well as by the abuses of power.

defs: A corrupt practice or custom; offense; crime; fault; as, the abuses in the civil service.
quotes: Abuse after disappeared without a struggle..

defs: Vituperative words; coarse, insulting speech; abusive language; virulent condemnation; reviling.
quotes: The two parties, after exchanging a good deal of abuse, came to blows.

defs: Violation; rape; as, abuse of a female child.
quotes: Or is it some abuse, and no such thing?
